summary: Cantrill argues that the computing industry fails to study its failures, unlike traditional engineering fields, and uses Robert Colwell's analysis of the Intel 432 as a prime example of valuable failure analysis. The 432 was a disastrous microprocessor that combined untested capability-based architecture with terrible execution—lacking registers, caches, and immediate values beyond 0 and 1. While the object model was blamed for its failure, Colwell's paper reveals the performance problems stemmed from implementation 'artifacts' caused by rigid adherence to novel concepts at the expense of fundamentals. Cantrill draws parallels to modern hardware transactional memory efforts, warning that hardware innovations requiring significant system software support risk repeating the 432's mistakes.
key_points: Computing systems lack a culture of studying failures, unlike traditional engineering disciplines
The Intel 432's failure was caused by both untested ideas and terrible execution, not just its novel object model
The 432 lacked fundamental features (registers, caches, immediate values) because resources went to the object model
Poor compiler implementation made every function call a context switch, costing 25-35% performance
Hardware transactional memory faces similar risks of disconnect between hardware innovation and software requirements
money_quotes: To understand systems, you must understand their pathologies – systems are most instructive when they fail.

Despite the fact that failure in our domain can be every bit as expensive (if not more so) than in traditional engineering domains, our failures do not (usually) involve loss of life or physical property and there is thus little public demand for us to study them – and a tremendous industrial bias for us to forget them as much and as quickly as possible.

The upshot of these decisions is that you have more code (because you have no immediates) accessing more memory (because you have no registers) that is dog-slow (because you have no data cache) that itself is not cached (because you have no instruction cache). Yee haw!
Intel designers believed that 0 and 1 'would cover nearly all the need for constants', a conclusion that Colwell (generously) describes as 'almost certainly in error.'

Its performance was 'dominated, in large part, by artifacts and not by concepts.' If there's one imperfection with Colwell's work, it's that he doesn't realize how convincingly he's made the case that these artifacts were induced by a rigid and foolish adherence to the concepts.
key_insight: When engineers become so enamored with novel concepts that they sacrifice fundamental requirements (like caches and registers), the resulting 'artifacts' of poor implementation become indistinguishable from flaws in the concepts themselves.
